entries.title: InQL - GraphQL Scanner: introspection, query generation, defense bypass
entries.body:
# InQL - GraphQL Scanner

**InQL** (by Doyensec) is the main extension for attacking GraphQL endpoints from Burp. It pulls the schema off the target via introspection, expands it into ready queries/mutations/subscriptions on its own and throws them into **Repeater** with a couple of clicks. It installs from the **BApp Store**; requires Java 17+ and works in both Community and Professional. As of 2026 the current branch is 6.x (version 6.1.2, updated February 2026), fully rewritten on the Montoya API - the old Python/Jython variant has long been dead.

## What it adds to the interface

After loading a separate top-level **InQL** tab appears with sub-tabs:

- **Scanner** - the main window. Here you feed in the endpoint URL (or a local file with the schema) and get a tree of all operations.
- **Batch Queries** - a constructor for batch queries: it packs several operations into one HTTP request to bypass broken rate limits and to check DoS vectors.
- **Engine Fingerprinting** - determines which GraphQL engine/server the backend runs on (by behavior and characteristic responses), so you can aim precisely at the known weaknesses of that family.

In addition, in Burp's native **HTTP message editor** (in **Proxy**, **Repeater**, **Intruder**) a **GraphQL (InQL)** tab is added to every GraphQL message - it shows the request body in a human-readable, highlighted form, and it is also convenient to edit it there without fighting escaped JSON.

## How to feed in the endpoint and read the schema

Two ways:

1. **Through Scanner directly.** The **InQL -> Scanner** tab, in the address field you type the endpoint URL (usually `/graphql`, `/api/graphql`, `/query`), set headers if needed and launch. InQL sends an introspection query, parses the response and builds a tree on the left: the sections **Queries**, **Mutations**, **Subscriptions**, and under them - specific operations with already filled-in arguments and nested fields.
2. **From intercepted traffic.** You caught a request to GraphQL in **Proxy** or opened it in **Repeater** - right-click -> an InQL menu item (for example **Send to InQL** / **Generate queries with InQL**). The extension itself takes the endpoint and headers from this request.

Generation can be controlled: the expansion depth (**query depth** - how deep InQL unrolls nested types; set it moderately, otherwise on recursive schemas queries balloon) and indentation for readability.

You send any operation from the tree onward with a right-click - **Send to Repeater** - and finish it by hand: substitute argument values, add/remove fields, hit authorization and IDOR at the level of individual fields.

## Introspection is disabled - what to do

Often in production introspection is turned off and an ordinary `__schema` query returns an error. InQL can reconstruct the schema even without it - a built-in **schema bruteforce** based on the Clairvoyance approach: it enumerates type/field names and from the text of server errors ("Did you mean ...", validator hints) gradually assembles the structure. This is slower and not 100% complete, but it lets you get a working tree where introspection is closed.

## Points of Interest - finding suspicious things in the schema

In **Scanner** there is a **Points of Interest (POI)** analysis - InQL combs through the schema and highlights potentially interesting things: fields and arguments with names like `password`, `token`, `secret`, `email`, `isAdmin`, `role`, file upload operations, debug/service queries, etc. POI categories are turned on/off with checkboxes so you do not drown in noise. This is a quick way to understand where the sensitive functionality lives in a large schema, and where to aim first.

## Circular queries (DoS) and batching

Two classes of problems for which InQL is especially useful:

- **Circular references / circular queries.** If types in the schema reference each other (for example `User -> posts -> author(User) -> posts -> ...`), you can build a deeply nested query that forces the server to recursively resolve fields and eat up CPU/memory - a classic GraphQL DoS when no depth limit is set. After analyzing the schema InQL highlights such potentially dangerous circular chains in the **Scanner** results so you can assemble a PoC from them. Test carefully and only within the allowed scope - this really takes services down.
- **Batching / aliases.** The **Batch Queries** tab gathers many operations into one request. This hits naive rate limits (the limit counts HTTP requests, while inside one you send hundreds of operations) - great for enumeration, brute-forcing OTP/passwords, a mass IDOR enumerator. If the batch itself is blocked, the same effect is achieved by many **aliases** of one mutation in one request (`a1: login(...) a2: login(...) ...`).

## Schema visualization

The schema can be opened in the built-in **GraphiQL** and **GraphQL Voyager** (InQL spins up local servers for them) - Voyager draws a graph of types on which those very cycles and links between entities are clearly visible.

## Practice

- Start with **Scanner** on `/graphql`; if it did not work - try alternative paths and **schema bruteforce** when introspection is closed.
- Run **Points of Interest** - it is a map of the schema's sensitive spots.
- Any operation -> into **Repeater** via the **GraphQL (InQL)** tab, and edit the fields there to check authorization/IDOR.
- **Custom headers per domain**: for authenticated testing set headers (for example `Authorization: Bearer ...`) per domain - InQL picks up the domain list from the observed traffic, so you will not have to type the token into every request.
- Run DoS chains and batch only where it is explicitly allowed by the program.

entries.title: Logger++ - end-to-end traffic log with powerful filters and grep
entries.body:
# Logger++

**Logger++** (NCC Group, author Corey Arthur) is an extended replacement for the built-in **Logger** tab: a multithreaded log that writes requests and responses from **all** Burp tools (Proxy, Repeater, Intruder, Scanner, Extender) into one table and gives you a proper filter language over it, grep by content, row highlighting and export. It installs from the **BApp Store**; the load rating is **High** (it eats a lot of memory - on large projects limit the log size). The current version is 3.20.0.

## What it adds to the interface

A separate top-level **Logger++** tab with sub-tabs:

- **View Logs** - the main table of records plus a request/response viewer. On top is a **Filter** field for the filter language, under the columns everything is clickable and sortable.
- **Filter Library** - saved named filters (see below about `#alias`).
- **Grep Values** - a separate grep engine over the whole log with value extraction from capture groups.
- **Options** - which tools to log, limits, autosave, export to Elasticsearch.

## Filter language

The basic form of an expression: `Field Operator Value`. Several conditions are joined with logic, parentheses for grouping are supported.

Available fields (main ones): `Request.Method`, `Request.Url`, `Request.Path`, `Request.Body`, `Request.Headers`, `Response.Status`, `Response.Body`, `Response.Headers`, `Response.InferredType` (the detected content type) and others.

Operators:
- `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - comparison/exact match;
- `CONTAINS` - substring;
- `MATCHES` - match by regex;
- `IN [..]` - membership in a list of values;
- logic: `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT), parentheses `( )`.

A string is taken in quotes; to specify a **regex** instead of a string, wrap it in slashes: `/.../`.

Examples of working expressions:

```text
Request.Method == "POST"
```
```text
Request.Body CONTAINS "password"
```
```text
Response.Status == 200 && Request.Method == "POST"
```
```text
Response.Status >= 500 || Response.Status == 403
```
```text
Response.InferredType IN ["json", "html", "xml"]
```
```text
Request.Path MATCHES /\/api\/v[0-9]+\//
```
```text
Request.Headers CONTAINS "Authorization: Bearer" && Response.Status == 200
```
```text
Response.Body CONTAINS "sql" && Response.Status >= 500
```

This same expression can be set as a **global** filter (what gets into the log at all) or as a **view** filter (what to show from what is already recorded).

## Filter Library and aliases

Save frequently used expressions in the **Filter Library** under a name - then substitute them into other filters as `#alias_name`. It is convenient to assemble a long filter from ready pieces, for example separately "only in-scope" + "only interesting codes".

## Colorize / row highlighting

A filter can be saved as a **color** one (Color Filter): rows matching the condition are highlighted with a chosen background/text color right in the table. This way in the general stream you immediately see, for example, all 500 responses, all requests with a token or all reflections of your marker. Several color filters work at once.

## Grep Values - value extraction

The **Grep Values** tab is not just a search but mass extraction. You set a regex, choose the area (**Search Request** / **Search Response** / both) and run it over the whole log. Logger++ pulls out the matches and capture-group values and consolidates them into **Unique Results** (unique values). A typical use: collect e-mails, CSRF tokens, internal IDs, versions, keys from all responses.

Example - emails:
```text
[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+
```
Example - extract values of the `id` parameter:
```text
[?&]id=([0-9]+)
```

## Columns and export

The set of columns is configured via right-click on the table header (method, status, length, MIME, source tool, time, etc.). Selected records are exported to **CSV** with a right-click; in **Options** the export of the log to **Elasticsearch** (for analysis in Kibana) and autosave are configured.

## Practice

- Keep a global filter on in-scope, otherwise the log balloons and eats memory.
- To analyze an incident/finding set a view filter like `Response.Status >= 500` or `Response.Body CONTAINS "<your marker>"` - faster than scrolling through the Proxy history.
- Color filters for 500s and for requests with `Authorization` save time on large volumes.
- **Grep Values** is the best way to collect tokens/emails/IDs from all traffic in one pass for a further attack.
- On long sessions limit the log size in **Options** so that Burp does not eat all the memory.

entries.title: JS Miner - finding secrets and endpoints in JS and source maps
entries.body:
# JS Miner

**JS Miner** is an extension for mining useful things from static files: primarily from JavaScript and JSON files. It searches for secrets (API keys, tokens, credentials), endpoints and paths, subdomains, cloud URLs, and also pulls sources out of **source map** (`.map`) files. Indispensable in bug-bounty reconnaissance: the frontend almost always leaks API addresses, keys and forgotten paths into JS. It installs from the **BApp Store**; as of 2026 the current branch is PortSwigger's (`PortSwigger/js-miner`).

## What and how it scans

Two modes:

**Passive (runs automatically)** as you browse the application and Burp loads static files:
- **Secrets** - keys/tokens/passwords in the code. To increase confidence, Shannon entropy is applied (long "random" strings are more likely to be a secret).
- **Endpoints** - paths and API calls (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE/PATCH) pulled out of JS.
- **Subdomains** - subdomains found in the code.
- **Cloud URLs** - links to cloud services (AWS, Azure, GCP, CloudFront, DigitalOcean, Oracle, Alibaba, Firebase, etc.).
- **Inline source maps** - if a source map is embedded right in the JS, it parses it in place.
- **Dependency Confusion** - detects internal npm packages and checks them against the public NPM registry for a potential dependency takeover.

**Active (runs manually)**:
- **Guessing source map files** - tries to reach `.map` files at presumed addresses (next to `.js`), even if there is no direct link to them. A found map lets you reconstruct the original code structure (TypeScript/original modules) - and that is a map of the application's endpoints and logic.

## How to launch - the context menu

The main path is a right-click in **Site map** (or on a record in **HTTP history**) on the needed host/folder/file -> the menu item **Extensions -> JS Miner**, and then one of the scan commands (for example **Run all passive scans**, **Run JS source mapper / guess source maps**, etc. - the exact items depend on the version). Convenient: generate traffic (walk around the site so that Burp pulls in all scripts), then point at the host root and run the scans all at once.

## Where to see the findings

JS Miner does not make a big tab of its own - it publishes results as Burp **Issues**. Look here:
- **Dashboard -> Issue activity** (in Professional) or the **Issues** tab in **Target -> Site map** - there records appear like "Secrets", "Endpoints", "Cloud URLs", "Source mapper", "Dependency Confusion".
- The progress of the tasks themselves - in **Dashboard -> Tasks** / the extension log (the **Extensions -> Output** tab for JS Miner).

Clicking an issue, you see in which file and on which line it was found, and the fragment itself - that is the lead for a further check.

## Reducing false positives

The entropy detector is noisy by nature: a long string (an asset hash, a CSS class, a base64 image) easily arrives as an "API Key". Therefore:
- Treat **Secrets** findings as candidates, not as fact - verify each one by eye and, where possible, in action (does the key really open something?).
- Pay special attention to keys with a recognizable prefix (`AKIA...` AWS, `AIza...` Google, `sk_live_...` Stripe, `ghp_...` GitHub) and to real secrets in JSON configs.
- **Endpoints** and **Cloud URLs** are more reliable than secrets - they are just extracted strings; put them to use right away (new paths into **Repeater**/the scanner, buckets - to an access check).
- JS Miner does not replace manual analysis: treat it as a quick first pass over static files, and finish off the interesting things by hand.

entries.title: Collaborator Everywhere - automatic injection of OAST payloads into traffic
entries.body:
# Collaborator Everywhere

**Collaborator Everywhere** is an extension that **on the fly** appends **Burp Collaborator** payloads to the traffic passing through Burp: it substitutes unique OAST addresses into headers (`Referer`, `X-Forwarded-For`, `X-Forwarded-Host`, `True-Client-IP`, `Origin`, etc.) and, if desired, into URL parameters of **in-scope** requests. After that all that remains is to wait: if some backend/proxy/SSRF handler resolves or pulls the substituted address, you get an out-of-band interaction - a passive way to catch **blind SSRF**, Host-header processing problems and other OAST bugs without attacking anything by hand. Requires **Burp Suite Professional** (Collaborator). As of 2026 the current one is **v2** (`PortSwigger/collaborator-everywhere-v2`) on the Montoya API.

## How it works

The extension passively intercepts requests as you simply browse the application through **Proxy**, and into each in-scope request it stitches Collaborator payloads according to its list of rules. No separate actions are needed - navigating the site scatters markers across a heap of input points on its own. When the target's server (or the infrastructure hidden behind it - a log collector, anti-fraud, an image renderer, a proxy) reaches out to the substituted address, Collaborator records the DNS/HTTP interaction, and the extension links it to the specific original request and header.

## What it adds to the interface (v2)

A separate **Collaborator Everywhere** tab with sub-tabs:

- **Interactions** - a table of all caught pingbacks: time, type (DNS/HTTP), source IP, which payload fired and from which request/header it came. Interactions are **stored in the project file**, that is, they survive a restart and travel with the project.
- **Payloads** - a list of injection rules: which headers/parameters and which payload templates are used. Records can be **added, edited (double-click on a cell), enabled/disabled with a checkbox, imported from a file**. **Placeholders** are supported that substitute real values from the request into the payload - in particular the values of the **Host**, **Origin**, **Referer** headers (useful when the target trusts/reflects these fields).
- **Settings** - the severity settings of the issues raised and the behavior for "own" interactions; stored in Burp's user settings.

In addition, in **Proxy -> HTTP history** the requests that produced an interaction are **highlighted and marked** with a note - it is convenient to filter the culprit requests by it.

## How to read interactions

1. Walk around the application through Burp (the more unique requests - the more markers placed).
2. Open the **Collaborator Everywhere -> Interactions** tab. Each row is the target's server that knocked on your address.
3. Type **DNS** - the application at least **resolved** the name (the most sensitive signal; often outgoing HTTP is closed while DNS gets through). Type **HTTP** - it actually went for content, this is a stronger confirmation of SSRF.
4. Look at **which header** fired and in **which request** - that is the injection point for the PoC. From the source IP and the headers of the incoming request you can see who exactly went (the front, an internal proxy, a third-party service).

For a finding the extension raises an **Issue** (in **Dashboard / Target**) with details: the delay between the original request and the pingback, the IP, the payload that fired.

## Pairing with active scanning

Collaborator Everywhere covers **headers and infrastructure points** that Burp's active scanner checks not so aggressively. The working scheme:

- The extension works **passively in the background** while you conduct ordinary manual/semi-automatic testing - it catches what you walked through with the browser.
- In parallel run the standard **active scan** on endpoints - it aims at body/URL parameters and confirms SSRF with its own payloads.
- A match from two sources (a header gave a pingback in Collaborator Everywhere, and the scanner found SSRF in a parameter) strengthens the finding and helps understand which input exactly is vulnerable.

## Practice

- Set the **scope** correctly - the extension works only on in-scope traffic; otherwise it is either silence or extra noise.
- First look at the **DNS** interactions: their presence in the absence of HTTP is not a failure but a signal that the target's outgoing web is shielded by a firewall.
- Through the **Host/Origin/Referer placeholders** hit applications that reflect or trust these headers.
- Remember: this is **Pro-only** and it really sends OAST traffic outward - do not run it on programs where out-of-band is forbidden.

entries.title: MCP Server - AI agent (Claude) access to Burp tools
entries.body:
# MCP Server

**MCP Server** is the official **PortSwigger** extension that brings up a **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** server inside Burp and through it lets AI agents (Claude and any MCP clients) control Burp programmatically: read the proxy history, send requests, throw them into **Repeater**/**Intruder**, work with the **Organizer**, generate Collaborator payloads and encode/decode data. In essence this is a "human + AI" bridge: you stay at the wheel in Burp's UI, while the model gets a strictly described set of tools and works over the same data. The extension is **new** and actively developing - as of 2026 the current version is 1.3.0 (updated May 2026), authors Daniel S. and Daniel Allen from PortSwigger. It installs from the **BApp Store**.

## How to enable it and what it listens on

After installation a top-level **MCP** tab appears. There:

- a checkbox to **enable/disable** the server (off by default - on its own it sticks out nothing externally);
- a checkbox **Enable tools that can edit your config** - separately allows tools capable of **changing Burp settings** (off by default; keep it off if not needed, so the agent does not edit the config);
- **host/port** settings.

It listens locally at **`http://127.0.0.1:9876`**. Two transports are supported:

- **SSE** (Server-Sent Events) - a direct HTTP connection to `http://127.0.0.1:9876` (the `.../sse` endpoint). Suitable for clients that can do SSE-MCP.
- **stdio** - a packaged **stdio proxy** (jar) is included that translates stdio<->SSE. It is needed for clients that understand only stdio-MCP - in particular **Claude Desktop**.

### Connecting Claude Desktop

Claude Desktop works only over stdio, so it launches the proxy jar that talks to Burp's SSE server. Something like this is added to the client config (`claude_desktop_config.json`) (the extension also has an auto-installer for this entry):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "burp": {
      "command": "<path to java>",
      "args": [
        "-jar",
        "<path to mcp-proxy-all.jar>",
        "--sse-url",
        "http://127.0.0.1:9876"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

## What it exposes (tools)

The agent is given a set of tools with strict JSON schemas and stable object identifiers (get-by-id/get-by-key, pagination for large lists). By capabilities this is:

- **Proxy history** - reading HTTP and WebSocket history, including with regex filtering.
- **Sending requests** - sending arbitrary HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 requests and getting the response.
- **Repeater** - creating Repeater tabs from a request (including HTTP/2).
- **Intruder** - sending a request to Intruder.
- **Organizer** - reading and stashing items into the Organizer.
- **Collaborator** - generating payloads and fetching interactions (only in **Professional**).
- **Codecs** - Base64 and URL encode/decode, generation of random strings.
- **Management** - toggling interception in Proxy and the state of the task engine; reading scanner issues; reading/writing user and project settings (the latter - only with the config-editing checkbox enabled).

## Security

- The server binds **only to `127.0.0.1`** - it is accessible only from the local machine; do not expose it externally (no 0.0.0.0). The local socket has no built-in authentication - protection rests on the fact that it listens only on localhost.
- Enable the server **only when you are actually working with an AI agent**, and disable it afterward - this is effectively a full remote control of Burp.
- Keep the **Enable tools that can edit your config** checkbox off without explicit necessity: otherwise the agent will be able to change Burp settings.
- Remember that everything the tool sends to an external AI service (request bodies, history) goes to the model provider and falls under its data-handling policy - do not feed sensitive traffic to the agent thoughtlessly.
- Do not confuse it with unofficial analogs (for example "Awesome MCP") - those are separate third-party extensions; here we mean PortSwigger's native extension.
